analysis graph
📄 example_climate_analysis
section

Overview

note

Note

A worked example of an exploratory climate notebook authored for the semantic renderer. The science is driven by synthetic data so it runs anywhere, but the structure — dataset → transform → diagnostic → figure → interpretation — mirrors a real reanalysis workflow.

section

Dataset

note

Dataset

The synthetic field is composed from the same ingredients a reanalysis
would contain, added explicitly:

$$z(x,t) = \bar{z}_{d(t)}(x) + E(t)\,P_E(x) + b(t)\,G(x) + \varepsilon(x,t)$$

  • $\bar{z}_d$ — day-of-year climatology: zonal gradient, wave-3 ridge, seasonal cycle
  • $E(t)\,P_E(x)$ — an ENSO-like slow mode with a fixed spatial pattern
  • $b(t)\,G(x)$ — episodic Tasman blocking events, a Gaussian blob switched on ~9% of days
  • $\varepsilon$ — weather noise with $\sigma = 22$ m

Because the truth is imposed, every diagnostic below has a known answer to
recover.

code

Imports & plotting style

%matplotlib inline
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(20260626)
plt.rcParams.update({
    'figure.dpi': 108, 'savefig.dpi': 108, 'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'axes.facecolor': 'white', 'font.size': 10,
    'axes.spines.top': False, 'axes.spines.right': False,
    'axes.titlesize': 11, 'axes.titleweight': 'semibold',
    'axes.labelcolor': '#33414f', 'text.color': '#16202b',
    'xtick.color': '#69788a', 'ytick.color': '#69788a',
    'axes.edgecolor': '#c9d3dc',
})
INK, CYAN, AMBER = '#16202b', '#1f7e93', '#cf9a4e'
transform

Grid & time axis

grid
lat = np.arange(-80, -19, 2.5)
lon = np.arange(0, 360, 2.5)
time = pd.date_range('2008-01-01', '2022-12-31', freq='D')
doy = time.dayofyear.values
LON, LAT = np.meshgrid(np.deg2rad(lon), lat)
transform

Synthetic climatology & variability modes

modes

The imposed truth: a wave-3 climatology with a seasonal cycle, an ENSO-like slow mode, and episodic Tasman blocking.

derives fromgrid
# stationary climatology: zonal gradient + wave-3, with a seasonal cycle
base = 5500 + 38 * (lat[:, None] + 50)
wave3 = 70 * np.cos(3 * LON + np.deg2rad(40)) * np.cos(np.deg2rad(LAT + 50))
clim_doy = np.empty((366, lat.size, lon.size), np.float32)
for d in range(366):
    seasonal = 55 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * (d - 15) / 365.25)
    clim_doy[d] = base + wave3 + seasonal * (lat[:, None] + 50) / 30

# ENSO-like index: two slow oscillations + red noise, standardised
t_yr = (time - time[0]).days.values / 365.25
enso = (np.sin(2*np.pi*t_yr/3.6) + 0.7*np.sin(2*np.pi*t_yr/5.2 + 1.1))
enso += np.cumsum(rng.standard_normal(time.size)) * 0.015
enso = pd.Series(enso).rolling(45, center=True, min_periods=1).mean().values
enso = (enso - enso.mean()) / enso.std()
enso_pattern = (np.exp(-((lat[:, None] + 45)**2)/450)
                * np.cos(2*np.deg2rad(lon)[None, :] - 0.6)) * 42

# episodic blocking south of Australia (~50S, 150E)
block_days = rng.random(time.size) < 0.09
blob = np.exp(-(((lat[:, None]+50)/9)**2) - (((lon[None, :]-150)/16)**2)) * 130
dataset

Daily Z500 field, Southern Hemisphere (synthetic)

z500

         6799.5483]]], shape=(5479, 25, 144), dtype=float32)
  • title :
    Synthetic SH Z500
    units :
    m
    source :
    demo

Fifteen years of daily 500 hPa geopotential height on a 2.5 deg grid from 80 deg S to 20 deg S. Climatology plus modes plus weather noise, assembled into one dataset.

derives fromgridmodes
anom = (enso[:, None, None] * enso_pattern[None]
        + block_days[:, None, None] * blob[None]
        + rng.standard_normal((time.size, lat.size, lon.size)) * 22
       ).astype(np.float32)
z = clim_doy[doy - 1] + anom

ds = xr.Dataset(
    {'z500': (('time', 'lat', 'lon'), z)},
    coords={'time': time, 'lat': lat, 'lon': lon},
    attrs={'title': 'Synthetic SH Z500', 'units': 'm', 'source': 'demo'},
)
ds.z500.attrs.update(long_name='500 hPa geopotential height', units='m')
ds
metric

Temporal & spatial coverage

coverage
period      : 2008-01-01 -> 2022-12-31 (5,479 days)
grid        : 25 lat x 144 lon  (2.5 deg)
domain      : -80 to -20 deg lat, full longitude
memory (z)  : 79 MB
derives fromz500
print(f"period      : {time[0]:%Y-%m-%d} -> {time[-1]:%Y-%m-%d} "
      f"({time.size:,} days)")
print(f"grid        : {lat.size} lat x {lon.size} lon  (2.5 deg)")
print(f"domain      : {lat.min():.0f} to {lat.max():.0f} deg lat, full longitude")
print(f"memory (z)  : {ds.z500.nbytes/1e6:.0f} MB")
section

Transforms

note

Transforms

Anomalies are defined against a day-of-year climatology,

$$z'(x,t) = z(x,t) - \bar{z}_{d(t)}(x), \qquad \bar{z}_d(x) = \frac{1}{N_y} \sum_{y=1}^{N_y} z(x, t_{y,d})$$

so the seasonal cycle drops out and what remains is the circulation
variability the diagnostics are after. The groupby('time.dayofyear')
idiom computes $\bar{z}_d$ and subtracts it in one pass.

transform

Day-of-year climatology

clim
derives fromz500
clim = ds.z500.groupby('time.dayofyear').mean('time')
transform

Seasonal-cycle removal -> daily anomalies

anom

  • long_name :
    Z500 anomaly
    units :
    m

Day-of-year climatology subtracted with a groupby, the standard first move before any circulation diagnostic. Everything downstream reads from these anomalies.

derives fromclim
z_anom = (ds.z500.groupby('time.dayofyear') - clim).rename('z500_anom')
z_anom.attrs.update(long_name='Z500 anomaly', units='m')
z_anom
section

Blocking diagnostics

note

Blocking diagnostics

Three complementary views of the same phenomenon: the summer climatology
that sets the stage, an interannual count of blocked days, and the
composite anomaly showing what a blocked day actually looks like.

figure

DJF mean Z500 and the climatological ridge

clim_map

Austral-summer mean. The wave-3 ridge poleward of Australia is the background state that blocking events amplify.

derives fromz500
djf = ds.z500.sel(time=ds['time.month'].isin([12, 1, 2])).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
cf = ax.contourf(lon, lat, djf, levels=16, cmap='viridis')
ax.contour(lon, lat, djf, levels=8, colors='white', linewidths=.4, alpha=.6)
ax.set_xlabel('longitude'); ax.set_ylabel('latitude')
ax.set_title('DJF mean 500 hPa height')
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
note

The blocking index

Blocking is measured from the box-mean anomaly over the Tasman sector,

$$B(t) = \left\langle z'(x,t) \right\rangle_{x \in \mathrm{box}}, \qquad \mathrm{box} = [58^\circ\mathrm{S},\,42^\circ\mathrm{S}] \times [135^\circ\mathrm{E},\,170^\circ\mathrm{E}]$$

A day counts as blocked when $B(t) > \sigma_B$, one standard deviation
of the box series, and the seasonal frequency is the blocked fraction of
austral-summer days:

$$f_y = \frac{ \#\{\, t \in \mathrm{DJF}_y : B(t) > \sigma_B \,\} }{ \#\,\mathrm{DJF}_y }$$

The $1\sigma$ threshold is the one free parameter — on real data the
composites below should be checked for sensitivity to it.

transform

Tasman sector box-mean series

tasman
derives fromanom
box = z_anom.sel(lat=slice(-58, -42), lon=slice(135, 170)).mean(['lat', 'lon'])
transform

Blocking threshold & DJF frequency

blockidx

A day is blocked when the box-mean anomaly exceeds +1 sigma; the frequency is the fraction of blocked DJF days per summer.

derives fromtasman
blocked = box > box.std()
freq = (blocked.sel(time=blocked['time.month'].isin([12, 1, 2]))
        .groupby('time.year').mean('time'))
figure

Tasman blocking frequency by austral summer

block_freq

Fraction of DJF days on which the box-mean anomaly over the Tasman sector exceeds +1 sigma. Interannual swings track the ENSO state.

derives fromblockidx
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.bar(freq.year, freq*100, color=CYAN, width=.66)
ax.axhline((freq*100).mean(), color=AMBER, lw=1.2, ls='--',
           label='period mean')
ax.set_xlabel('year'); ax.set_ylabel('blocked DJF days (%)')
ax.set_title('Tasman blocking frequency'); ax.legend(frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
metric

Blocking summary statistics

block_stat
mean DJF blocking frequency :   9.3 %
most blocked summer         : 2013 (14%)
least blocked summer        : 2018 (2%)

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/xarray/core/dataarray.py:6439: FutureWarning: Behaviour of argmin/argmax with neither dim nor axis argument will change to return a dict of indices of each dimension. To get a single, flat index, please use np.argmin(da.data) or np.argmax(da.data) instead of da.argmin() or da.argmax().
  result = self.variable.argmax(dim, axis, keep_attrs, skipna)
/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/xarray/core/dataarray.py:6318: FutureWarning: Behaviour of argmin/argmax with neither dim nor axis argument will change to return a dict of indices of each dimension. To get a single, flat index, please use np.argmin(da.data) or np.argmax(da.data) instead of da.argmin() or da.argmax().
  result = self.variable.argmin(dim, axis, keep_attrs, skipna)
derives fromblockidx
print(f"mean DJF blocking frequency : {float(freq.mean())*100:5.1f} %")
print(f"most blocked summer         : {int(freq.year[freq.argmax()])} "
      f"({float(freq.max())*100:.0f}%)")
print(f"least blocked summer        : {int(freq.year[freq.argmin()])} "
      f"({float(freq.min())*100:.0f}%)")
figure

Composite Z500 anomaly on blocked days

block_comp

Mean anomaly over all blocked days. The localised positive centre is the blocking high. Drawn with the shared map helper, folded in as step 1.

derives fromanomblockidx
1shared map helper
def plot_anom_map(da, ax, title, vmax=None):
    """Filled SH anomaly map on a lon-lat axis; shared by the composites."""
    vmax = vmax or float(abs(da).quantile(.98))
    pc = ax.pcolormesh(da.lon, da.lat, da, cmap='RdBu_r',
                       vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading='auto')
    ax.set_xlabel('longitude'); ax.set_ylabel('latitude')
    ax.set_title(title)
    return pc
2plot
comp = z_anom.sel(time=blocked).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
pc = plot_anom_map(comp, ax, 'Blocked-day composite anomaly')
ax.plot(150, -50, marker='x', color='k', ms=8, mew=2)
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
note

Zonal-mean evolution

Averaging the anomaly around each latitude circle, $\langle z' \rangle_{\lambda}(\phi, t)$, collapses the field onto a time–latitude plane. A 30-day running mean strips the synoptic noise so the slow, ENSO-paced pulses stand out.

figure

Zonal-mean Z500 anomaly (time-latitude)

zonal_hov

A Hovmoller of the zonally averaged anomaly. The point of grouping: the zonal-mean and smoothing prep folds behind this one figure as a numbered step, instead of taking up its own card.

derives fromanom
1zonal mean + 30-day smoothing
zm = z_anom.mean('lon')                         # zonal-mean anomaly
zm = zm.rolling(time=30, center=True).mean()    # 30-day running mean
zm_mon = zm.resample(time='1MS').mean()         # monthly, for a clean plot
print('zonal-mean field:', dict(zm_mon.sizes))
zonal-mean field: {'time': 180, 'lat': 25}
2plot Hovmoller
vmax = float(abs(zm_mon).quantile(.98))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.4))
pc = ax.pcolormesh(zm_mon.time.values, lat, zm_mon.T.values,
                   cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading='auto')
ax.set_ylabel('latitude'); ax.set_xlabel('time')
ax.set_title('Zonal-mean anomaly Hovmoller')
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
section

ENSO diagnostics

transform

Project anomalies onto the ENSO pattern

enso_idx

Least-squares projection of the daily anomalies onto the ENSO spatial mode, standardised to sigma units, then monthly-averaged.

derives fromanommodes
pat = xr.DataArray(enso_pattern, coords={'lat': lat, 'lon': lon},
                   dims=['lat', 'lon'])
idx = ((z_anom * pat).sum(['lat', 'lon'])
       / (pat**2).sum(['lat', 'lon']))
idx = (idx - idx.mean()) / idx.std()
idx_m = idx.resample(time='1MS').mean()
note

Index construction

The ENSO index is the least-squares projection of each day's anomaly map
onto the fixed spatial pattern $P(x)$:

$$I(t) = \frac{ \sum_x z'(x,t)\, P(x) }{ \sum_x P(x)^2 }, \qquad \tilde{I}(t) = \frac{I(t) - \mu_I}{\sigma_I}$$

$I(t)$ is the amplitude that, multiplied by the pattern, best reconstructs
that day's anomaly field. Standardising to $\tilde{I}$ puts the phase
thresholds in sigma units; monthly averaging removes the daily jitter.

figure

ENSO index from the leading SH anomaly mode

nino34

The standardised index, monthly. Warm and cool phases are shaded at the +/- 0.5 sigma thresholds.

derives fromenso_idx
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.fill_between(idx_m.time, 0, idx_m.where(idx_m > .5),
                color='#c0504d', alpha=.85, label='warm')
ax.fill_between(idx_m.time, 0, idx_m.where(idx_m < -.5),
                color='#3a7ca5', alpha=.85, label='cool')
ax.plot(idx_m.time, idx_m, color=INK, lw=.7)
ax.axhline(0, color='#69788a', lw=.6)
ax.set_ylabel('sigma'); ax.set_title('ENSO index (monthly)')
ax.legend(frameon=False, ncol=2)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
figure

Warm-phase teleconnection composite

enso_comp

Mean anomaly across warm-phase months (index > +0.8 sigma). Reuses the same map helper as the blocking composite, stacked under this figure too.

derives fromanomenso_idx
1shared map helper
def plot_anom_map(da, ax, title, vmax=None):
    """Filled SH anomaly map on a lon-lat axis; shared by the composites."""
    vmax = vmax or float(abs(da).quantile(.98))
    pc = ax.pcolormesh(da.lon, da.lat, da, cmap='RdBu_r',
                       vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading='auto')
    ax.set_xlabel('longitude'); ax.set_ylabel('latitude')
    ax.set_title(title)
    return pc
2plot
warm = idx > 0.8
tele = z_anom.sel(time=warm).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
pc = plot_anom_map(tele, ax, 'Warm-phase composite anomaly')
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
figure

Power spectrum of the ENSO index

enso_spec

Periodogram of the monthly index. Variance concentrates in the 3-6 year band (shaded), as expected for the synthetic forcing.

derives fromenso_idx
x = (idx_m - idx_m.mean()).values
freqs = np.fft.rfftfreq(x.size, d=1/12)  # cycles per year
power = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x))**2
mask = freqs > 0
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.axvspan(1/6, 1/3, color=AMBER, alpha=.16)
ax.plot(1/freqs[mask], power[mask], color=CYAN, lw=1.4)
ax.set_xlim(0, 9); ax.set_xlabel('period (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('power'); ax.set_title('ENSO index spectrum')
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
section

Interpretation

note

Interpretation

The diagnostics hang together. The DJF climatology sets up a wave-3 ridge near the Tasman sector; blocked days compositie into a localised high in exactly that region; and the years with the most blocking line up with the warm phase of the ENSO index. The teleconnection composite recovers the same spatial mode the index was built to track, so the index and the blocking metric are measuring consistent physics rather than noise.

note

Caveats

This is synthetic data with an imposed relationship, so the ENSO–blocking link here is true by construction. On reanalysis the same pipeline would need significance testing on the composites and a sensitivity check on the +1 sigma blocking threshold before any of this could be read as a real teleconnection.

markdown

Southern Hemisphere blocking & ENSO diagnostics

A worked example of an exploratory climate notebook authored for the semantic renderer. The science is driven by synthetic data so it runs anywhere, but the structure — dataset → transform → diagnostic → figure → interpretation — mirrors a real reanalysis workflow.

markdown

Dataset

The synthetic field is composed from the same ingredients a reanalysis
would contain, added explicitly:

$$z(x,t) = \bar{z}_{d(t)}(x) + E(t)\,P_E(x) + b(t)\,G(x) + \varepsilon(x,t)$$

  • $\bar{z}_d$ — day-of-year climatology: zonal gradient, wave-3 ridge, seasonal cycle
  • $E(t)\,P_E(x)$ — an ENSO-like slow mode with a fixed spatial pattern
  • $b(t)\,G(x)$ — episodic Tasman blocking events, a Gaussian blob switched on ~9% of days
  • $\varepsilon$ — weather noise with $\sigma = 22$ m

Because the truth is imposed, every diagnostic below has a known answer to
recover.

In [1]
#| title: Imports & plotting style
%matplotlib inline
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(20260626)
plt.rcParams.update({
    'figure.dpi': 108, 'savefig.dpi': 108, 'figure.facecolor': 'white',
    'axes.facecolor': 'white', 'font.size': 10,
    'axes.spines.top': False, 'axes.spines.right': False,
    'axes.titlesize': 11, 'axes.titleweight': 'semibold',
    'axes.labelcolor': '#33414f', 'text.color': '#16202b',
    'xtick.color': '#69788a', 'ytick.color': '#69788a',
    'axes.edgecolor': '#c9d3dc',
})
INK, CYAN, AMBER = '#16202b', '#1f7e93', '#cf9a4e'
In [ ]
#| display: transform
#| id: grid
#| title: Grid & time axis
lat = np.arange(-80, -19, 2.5)
lon = np.arange(0, 360, 2.5)
time = pd.date_range('2008-01-01', '2022-12-31', freq='D')
doy = time.dayofyear.values
LON, LAT = np.meshgrid(np.deg2rad(lon), lat)
In [ ]
#| display: transform
#| id: modes
#| depends: grid
#| title: Synthetic climatology & variability modes
#| caption: The imposed truth: a wave-3 climatology with a seasonal cycle, an ENSO-like slow mode, and episodic Tasman blocking.
# stationary climatology: zonal gradient + wave-3, with a seasonal cycle
base = 5500 + 38 * (lat[:, None] + 50)
wave3 = 70 * np.cos(3 * LON + np.deg2rad(40)) * np.cos(np.deg2rad(LAT + 50))
clim_doy = np.empty((366, lat.size, lon.size), np.float32)
for d in range(366):
    seasonal = 55 * np.cos(2 * np.pi * (d - 15) / 365.25)
    clim_doy[d] = base + wave3 + seasonal * (lat[:, None] + 50) / 30

# ENSO-like index: two slow oscillations + red noise, standardised
t_yr = (time - time[0]).days.values / 365.25
enso = (np.sin(2*np.pi*t_yr/3.6) + 0.7*np.sin(2*np.pi*t_yr/5.2 + 1.1))
enso += np.cumsum(rng.standard_normal(time.size)) * 0.015
enso = pd.Series(enso).rolling(45, center=True, min_periods=1).mean().values
enso = (enso - enso.mean()) / enso.std()
enso_pattern = (np.exp(-((lat[:, None] + 45)**2)/450)
                * np.cos(2*np.deg2rad(lon)[None, :] - 0.6)) * 42

# episodic blocking south of Australia (~50S, 150E)
block_days = rng.random(time.size) < 0.09
blob = np.exp(-(((lat[:, None]+50)/9)**2) - (((lon[None, :]-150)/16)**2)) * 130
In [2]
#| display: dataset
#| id: z500
#| depends: grid, modes
#| title: Daily Z500 field, Southern Hemisphere (synthetic)
#| caption: Fifteen years of daily 500 hPa geopotential height on a 2.5 deg grid from 80 deg S to 20 deg S. Climatology plus modes plus weather noise, assembled into one dataset.
anom = (enso[:, None, None] * enso_pattern[None]
        + block_days[:, None, None] * blob[None]
        + rng.standard_normal((time.size, lat.size, lon.size)) * 22
       ).astype(np.float32)
z = clim_doy[doy - 1] + anom

ds = xr.Dataset(
    {'z500': (('time', 'lat', 'lon'), z)},
    coords={'time': time, 'lat': lat, 'lon': lon},
    attrs={'title': 'Synthetic SH Z500', 'units': 'm', 'source': 'demo'},
)
ds.z500.attrs.update(long_name='500 hPa geopotential height', units='m')
ds

In [3]
#| display: metric
#| id: coverage
#| depends: z500
#| title: Temporal & spatial coverage
print(f"period      : {time[0]:%Y-%m-%d} -> {time[-1]:%Y-%m-%d} "
      f"({time.size:,} days)")
print(f"grid        : {lat.size} lat x {lon.size} lon  (2.5 deg)")
print(f"domain      : {lat.min():.0f} to {lat.max():.0f} deg lat, full longitude")
print(f"memory (z)  : {ds.z500.nbytes/1e6:.0f} MB")
period      : 2008-01-01 -> 2022-12-31 (5,479 days)
grid        : 25 lat x 144 lon  (2.5 deg)
domain      : -80 to -20 deg lat, full longitude
memory (z)  : 79 MB
markdown

Transforms

Anomalies are defined against a day-of-year climatology,

$$z'(x,t) = z(x,t) - \bar{z}_{d(t)}(x), \qquad \bar{z}_d(x) = \frac{1}{N_y} \sum_{y=1}^{N_y} z(x, t_{y,d})$$

so the seasonal cycle drops out and what remains is the circulation
variability the diagnostics are after. The groupby('time.dayofyear')
idiom computes $\bar{z}_d$ and subtracts it in one pass.

In [ ]
#| display: transform
#| id: clim
#| depends: z500
#| title: Day-of-year climatology
clim = ds.z500.groupby('time.dayofyear').mean('time')
In [4]
#| display: transform
#| id: anom
#| depends: clim
#| title: Seasonal-cycle removal -> daily anomalies
#| caption: Day-of-year climatology subtracted with a groupby, the standard first move before any circulation diagnostic. Everything downstream reads from these anomalies.
z_anom = (ds.z500.groupby('time.dayofyear') - clim).rename('z500_anom')
z_anom.attrs.update(long_name='Z500 anomaly', units='m')
z_anom
<xarray.DataArray 'z500_anom' (time: 5479, lat: 25, lon: 144)> Size: 79MB
array([[[-32.958984  ,  -1.4008789 ,  24.528809  , ..., -26.481445  ,
          12.447754  , -31.006836  ],
        [  9.697754  , -26.5625    , -13.802246  , ..., -14.928711  ,
         -18.674316  ,  11.130371  ],

         -21.496094  ,   4.8754883 ],
...

          28.506836  ,  28.219727  ],

       [[ 27.333984  , -12.894531  ,   3.1279297 , ...,   3.0966797 ,

          17.161621  ,  19.255371  ],

        [ 68.359375  ,  18.163086  ,  52.73584   , ...,  20.04248   ,
          28.811035  ,  33.554688  ],

          14.447266  ,  17.998535  ],

          -7.897949  ,  48.46582   ]]],
      shape=(5479, 25, 144), dtype=float32)

  • long_name :

    m
In [5]
#| id: maphelper
#| step: shared map helper
def plot_anom_map(da, ax, title, vmax=None):
    """Filled SH anomaly map on a lon-lat axis; shared by the composites."""
    vmax = vmax or float(abs(da).quantile(.98))
    pc = ax.pcolormesh(da.lon, da.lat, da, cmap='RdBu_r',
                       vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading='auto')
    ax.set_xlabel('longitude'); ax.set_ylabel('latitude')
    ax.set_title(title)
    return pc
markdown

Blocking diagnostics

Three complementary views of the same phenomenon: the summer climatology
that sets the stage, an interannual count of blocked days, and the
composite anomaly showing what a blocked day actually looks like.

In [6]
#| subsection: Climatology
#| display: figure
#| id: clim_map
#| depends: z500
#| title: DJF mean Z500 and the climatological ridge
#| caption: Austral-summer mean. The wave-3 ridge poleward of Australia is the background state that blocking events amplify.
djf = ds.z500.sel(time=ds['time.month'].isin([12, 1, 2])).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
cf = ax.contourf(lon, lat, djf, levels=16, cmap='viridis')
ax.contour(lon, lat, djf, levels=8, colors='white', linewidths=.4, alpha=.6)
ax.set_xlabel('longitude'); ax.set_ylabel('latitude')
ax.set_title('DJF mean 500 hPa height')
fig.colorbar(cf, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
markdown

The blocking index

Blocking is measured from the box-mean anomaly over the Tasman sector,

$$B(t) = \left\langle z'(x,t) \right\rangle_{x \in \mathrm{box}}, \qquad \mathrm{box} = [58^\circ\mathrm{S},\,42^\circ\mathrm{S}] \times [135^\circ\mathrm{E},\,170^\circ\mathrm{E}]$$

A day counts as blocked when $B(t) > \sigma_B$, one standard deviation
of the box series, and the seasonal frequency is the blocked fraction of
austral-summer days:

$$f_y = \frac{ \#\{\, t \in \mathrm{DJF}_y : B(t) > \sigma_B \,\} }{ \#\,\mathrm{DJF}_y }$$

The $1\sigma$ threshold is the one free parameter — on real data the
composites below should be checked for sensitivity to it.

In [ ]
#| subsection: Blocking frequency
#| display: transform
#| id: tasman
#| depends: anom
#| title: Tasman sector box-mean series
box = z_anom.sel(lat=slice(-58, -42), lon=slice(135, 170)).mean(['lat', 'lon'])
In [ ]
#| display: transform
#| id: blockidx
#| depends: tasman
#| title: Blocking threshold & DJF frequency
#| caption: A day is blocked when the box-mean anomaly exceeds +1 sigma; the frequency is the fraction of blocked DJF days per summer.
blocked = box > box.std()
freq = (blocked.sel(time=blocked['time.month'].isin([12, 1, 2]))
        .groupby('time.year').mean('time'))
In [7]
#| display: figure
#| id: block_freq
#| depends: blockidx
#| title: Tasman blocking frequency by austral summer
#| caption: Fraction of DJF days on which the box-mean anomaly over the Tasman sector exceeds +1 sigma. Interannual swings track the ENSO state.
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.bar(freq.year, freq*100, color=CYAN, width=.66)
ax.axhline((freq*100).mean(), color=AMBER, lw=1.2, ls='--',
           label='period mean')
ax.set_xlabel('year'); ax.set_ylabel('blocked DJF days (%)')
ax.set_title('Tasman blocking frequency'); ax.legend(frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [8]
#| subsection: Blocking frequency
#| display: metric
#| id: block_stat
#| depends: blockidx
#| title: Blocking summary statistics
print(f"mean DJF blocking frequency : {float(freq.mean())*100:5.1f} %")
print(f"most blocked summer         : {int(freq.year[freq.argmax()])} "
      f"({float(freq.max())*100:.0f}%)")
print(f"least blocked summer        : {int(freq.year[freq.argmin()])} "
      f"({float(freq.min())*100:.0f}%)")
mean DJF blocking frequency :   9.3 %
most blocked summer         : 2013 (14%)
least blocked summer        : 2018 (2%)

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/xarray/core/dataarray.py:6439: FutureWarning: Behaviour of argmin/argmax with neither dim nor axis argument will change to return a dict of indices of each dimension. To get a single, flat index, please use np.argmin(da.data) or np.argmax(da.data) instead of da.argmin() or da.argmax().
  result = self.variable.argmax(dim, axis, keep_attrs, skipna)
/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/xarray/core/dataarray.py:6318: FutureWarning: Behaviour of argmin/argmax with neither dim nor axis argument will change to return a dict of indices of each dimension. To get a single, flat index, please use np.argmin(da.data) or np.argmax(da.data) instead of da.argmin() or da.argmax().
  result = self.variable.argmin(dim, axis, keep_attrs, skipna)
In [9]
#| subsection: Regional composite
#| display: figure
#| id: block_comp
#| depends: anom, blockidx
#| stack: maphelper
#| title: Composite Z500 anomaly on blocked days
#| caption: Mean anomaly over all blocked days. The localised positive centre is the blocking high. Drawn with the shared map helper, folded in as step 1.
comp = z_anom.sel(time=blocked).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
pc = plot_anom_map(comp, ax, 'Blocked-day composite anomaly')
ax.plot(150, -50, marker='x', color='k', ms=8, mew=2)
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
markdown

Zonal-mean evolution

Averaging the anomaly around each latitude circle, $\langle z' \rangle_{\lambda}(\phi, t)$, collapses the field onto a time–latitude plane. A 30-day running mean strips the synoptic noise so the slow, ENSO-paced pulses stand out.

In [10]
#| group: fig_zonal
#| order: 1
#| step: zonal mean + 30-day smoothing
zm = z_anom.mean('lon')                         # zonal-mean anomaly
zm = zm.rolling(time=30, center=True).mean()    # 30-day running mean
zm_mon = zm.resample(time='1MS').mean()         # monthly, for a clean plot
print('zonal-mean field:', dict(zm_mon.sizes))
zonal-mean field: {'time': 180, 'lat': 25}
In [11]
#| group: fig_zonal
#| order: 2
#| step: plot Hovmoller
#| display: figure
#| id: zonal_hov
#| depends: anom
#| title: Zonal-mean Z500 anomaly (time-latitude)
#| caption: A Hovmoller of the zonally averaged anomaly. The point of grouping: the zonal-mean and smoothing prep folds behind this one figure as a numbered step, instead of taking up its own card.
vmax = float(abs(zm_mon).quantile(.98))
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.4))
pc = ax.pcolormesh(zm_mon.time.values, lat, zm_mon.T.values,
                   cmap='RdBu_r', vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading='auto')
ax.set_ylabel('latitude'); ax.set_xlabel('time')
ax.set_title('Zonal-mean anomaly Hovmoller')
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [ ]
#| section: ENSO diagnostics
#| display: transform
#| id: enso_idx
#| depends: anom, modes
#| title: Project anomalies onto the ENSO pattern
#| caption: Least-squares projection of the daily anomalies onto the ENSO spatial mode, standardised to sigma units, then monthly-averaged.
pat = xr.DataArray(enso_pattern, coords={'lat': lat, 'lon': lon},
                   dims=['lat', 'lon'])
idx = ((z_anom * pat).sum(['lat', 'lon'])
       / (pat**2).sum(['lat', 'lon']))
idx = (idx - idx.mean()) / idx.std()
idx_m = idx.resample(time='1MS').mean()
markdown

Index construction

The ENSO index is the least-squares projection of each day's anomaly map
onto the fixed spatial pattern $P(x)$:

$$I(t) = \frac{ \sum_x z'(x,t)\, P(x) }{ \sum_x P(x)^2 }, \qquad \tilde{I}(t) = \frac{I(t) - \mu_I}{\sigma_I}$$

$I(t)$ is the amplitude that, multiplied by the pattern, best reconstructs
that day's anomaly field. Standardising to $\tilde{I}$ puts the phase
thresholds in sigma units; monthly averaging removes the daily jitter.

In [12]
#| display: figure
#| id: nino34
#| depends: enso_idx
#| title: ENSO index from the leading SH anomaly mode
#| caption: The standardised index, monthly. Warm and cool phases are shaded at the +/- 0.5 sigma thresholds.
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.fill_between(idx_m.time, 0, idx_m.where(idx_m > .5),
                color='#c0504d', alpha=.85, label='warm')
ax.fill_between(idx_m.time, 0, idx_m.where(idx_m < -.5),
                color='#3a7ca5', alpha=.85, label='cool')
ax.plot(idx_m.time, idx_m, color=INK, lw=.7)
ax.axhline(0, color='#69788a', lw=.6)
ax.set_ylabel('sigma'); ax.set_title('ENSO index (monthly)')
ax.legend(frameon=False, ncol=2)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [13]
#| display: figure
#| subsection: Teleconnections
#| id: enso_comp
#| depends: anom, enso_idx
#| stack: maphelper
#| title: Warm-phase teleconnection composite
#| caption: Mean anomaly across warm-phase months (index > +0.8 sigma). Reuses the same map helper as the blocking composite, stacked under this figure too.
warm = idx > 0.8
tele = z_anom.sel(time=warm).mean('time')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.5))
pc = plot_anom_map(tele, ax, 'Warm-phase composite anomaly')
fig.colorbar(pc, ax=ax, label='m', pad=.02, shrink=.92)
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
In [14]
#| display: figure
#| id: enso_spec
#| depends: enso_idx
#| title: Power spectrum of the ENSO index
#| caption: Periodogram of the monthly index. Variance concentrates in the 3-6 year band (shaded), as expected for the synthetic forcing.
x = (idx_m - idx_m.mean()).values
freqs = np.fft.rfftfreq(x.size, d=1/12)  # cycles per year
power = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(x))**2
mask = freqs > 0
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.0))
ax.axvspan(1/6, 1/3, color=AMBER, alpha=.16)
ax.plot(1/freqs[mask], power[mask], color=CYAN, lw=1.4)
ax.set_xlim(0, 9); ax.set_xlabel('period (years)')
ax.set_ylabel('power'); ax.set_title('ENSO index spectrum')
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
markdown

Interpretation

The diagnostics hang together. The DJF climatology sets up a wave-3 ridge near the Tasman sector; blocked days compositie into a localised high in exactly that region; and the years with the most blocking line up with the warm phase of the ENSO index. The teleconnection composite recovers the same spatial mode the index was built to track, so the index and the blocking metric are measuring consistent physics rather than noise.

markdown

Caveats

This is synthetic data with an imposed relationship, so the ENSO–blocking link here is true by construction. On reanalysis the same pipeline would need significance testing on the composites and a sensitivity check on the +1 sigma blocking threshold before any of this could be read as a real teleconnection.

PlotLine

Open a notebook

Drop .ipynb files anywhere in this window, or browse for them. Each notebook opens as a tab; presentations can mix cards from every open tab.

How to use · GitHub · Support ♥
How to use GitHub ↗

What this is

A figure-first view of executed Jupyter notebooks. Instead of a wall of cells, you get the scientific structure: figures, datasets and notes as cards, code folded underneath, sections in a sidebar, and a provenance graph of how each result derives from the data. On top of that sits a presentation builder: turn any notebooks into slides and present straight from the browser.

Everything runs locally — in the web version, notebooks are processed in your browser and never uploaded anywhere.

Open notebooks

  • Drag & drop one or more .ipynb files anywhere onto the window.
  • + Open (top left) — a file picker, or paste a URL to a notebook (GitHub links are converted automatically).
  • Notebooks must be executed (run once in Jupyter so outputs are saved) — nothing is re-run here.
  • Every notebook is a tab: click to switch, ↻ re-reads it after you re-run it in Jupyter, ✕ closes.

Reading a notebook

  • Hide figures / markup / code (top bar) filter every tab at once; Show code unfolds ALL code, including the code tucked under each figure. Hidden cards become slim stubs that expand when clicked.
  • Raw notebook flips the current tab to the notebook exactly as authored — cells in order, directives visible — so you can always see where a title or caption came from.
  • The left sidebar navigates sections; the analysis graph at its foot jumps to any node. derives from chips under a card jump to its inputs.
  • Show code under a figure tells the whole story: every upstream cell (load → transform → plot) in execution order, traced automatically from the code's variables.

Make notebooks render better (optional)

Add #| directive lines to the top of a code cell; they are parsed and hidden from display. Everything works without them — they are how you take control:

#| title:card title (else inferred from the first comment)
#| caption:interpretation text under the output
#| section:start a section (or use a markdown ## heading)
#| id:stable name; makes the cell a node in the graph and a reliable slide anchor
#| depends: a, bdeclare inputs; draws the graph edges
#| display:force a card type: figure, dataset, metric, text, code, hidden
#| group: / #| stack:fold several cells under one figure (see the README for details)

Presentations

  • The left rail lists presentations under a Documents button — exactly one is active, so that button is always the way back. New starts one; « shrinks or hides the rail.
  • In the builder, pick a slide layout (diagrams: full, halves, rows, quarters, a title slide, or a blank canvas), click a pane, then click any card in the document to place it — from any open tab, so one deck can mix several notebooks.
  • ✎ Edit slide opens a PowerPoint-style editor: + Text, + Arrow, + Box, and + Cell — a draggable, resizable frame that holds any notebook card (click it, pick a card, swap later with ⇄ Replace). Select anything for colours, text size, line thickness, dash and fill.
  • ▶ Present plays full screen. Arrow keys ←/→ move through the story; on slides with code, ↓ descends the code trail — every cell that made the figure, one per screen, in execution order — and ↑ climbs back out.

Saving

  • Edits autosave as drafts in your browser as you work.
  • Desktop app: presentations autosave to plotline_project.json next to where you launched it, along with your open tabs.
  • Anywhere: File → Download JSON saves a deck as a file on your machine; File → Load deck JSON brings it back — later, or on another computer.
  • Decks are robust to notebook edits: slides reference cells by stable ids, never position — re-upload an edited notebook and everything still resolves; a deleted cell just leaves an empty frame you can refill.

Run it locally

The whole tool is one Python file with no dependencies. For daily use — local file browsing, project files, session restore: pip install the repo and run plotline, or just download semantic_render.py and run python semantic_render.py.

Drop .ipynb files to open
edit slide T box
📌 Click a card in the notebook to place it in the slide
